text: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments
textPreview: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments

alt: AI Playbook for Public Health logo

text:
Welcome participants to the Accessibility and Language Access lesson.
Explain that this deck is designed for staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, or cross-program discussion.
Frame the topic in plain language: AI-supported public health materials, tools, dashboards, chatbots, translations, and workflows should be reviewed for disability access, language access, readability, cultural context, and usability.
Invite participants to think about where this topic already appears in their department's workflows, vendor tools, data systems, communications, or planning conversations.
AI-supported public health materials, tools, dashboards, chatbots, translations, and workflows should be reviewed for disability access, language access, readability, cultural context, and usability.
textPreview: AI-supported public health materials, tools, dashboards, chatbots, translations, and workflows should be reviewed for disability access, ...

text: Accessible and language-appropriate AI-supported communication is essential for equitable public health service delivery and public trust.
textPreview: Accessible and language-appropriate AI-supported communication is essential for equitable public health service delivery and public trust.

text: AI can create or worsen access barriers if outputs are not tested with people who rely on accessible formats, language support, or non-digital pathways.
textPreview: AI can create or worsen access barriers if outputs are not tested with people who rely on accessible formats, language support, or non-di...

text:
Use this slide in the Accessibility and Language Access training module.
Use this slide to explain the practical public health reason for learning this topic before AI tools are approved or deployed.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 4, 7, 10, 11, 13 and Tools 3, 19, 20, 22, 29, 41, 47.
Emphasize the core guardrail: AI can create or worsen access barriers if outputs are not tested with people who rely on accessible formats, language support, or non-digital pathways.
Ask participants how this topic could affect their own program area, staff roles, community relationships, data, or public communications.
By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:
textPreview: By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:

text:
• Explain Accessibility and Language Access in plain language.
• Identify appropriate public health uses and limits.
• Describe human review, governance, equity, privacy, and monitoring expectations.
• Connect the topic to the related plays and tools.

text:
Use this slide in the Accessibility and Language Access training module.
Walk through the objectives and connect each one to a practical implementation decision.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 4, 7, 10, 11, 13 and Tools 3, 19, 20, 22, 29, 41, 47.
Explain that participants do not need technical expertise to use this material. The goal is enough shared understanding to participate in governance, readiness assessment, use case review, procurement, deployment, or monitoring.

AI can support translation, plain-language rewriting, alternative formats, and communication drafts, but those outputs still need human review. Accessibility and language access should be part of planning, testing, communications approval, and monitoring.

text:
Use this slide in the Accessibility and Language Access training module.
Use this slide to provide the narrative overview and definition before moving into detailed application.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 4, 7, 10, 11, 13 and Tools 3, 19, 20, 22, 29, 41, 47.
Ask the group to restate the topic in their own words.

AI-generated public health messages, FAQs, chatbot responses, translations, summaries, forms, dashboards, and outreach lists. Reading level, clarity, disability access, screen-reader compatibility, captions, plain-language structure, cultural.
textPreview: AI-generated public health messages, FAQs, chatbot responses, translations, summaries, forms, dashboards, and outreach lists. Reading lev...

text: AI translation should not be used for high-stakes public messaging without qualified human review. Departments should review dialect, cultural context, health terminology, and risk communication accuracy. Staff should document which languages.
textPreview: AI translation should not be used for high-stakes public messaging without qualified human review. Departments should review dialect, cul...

text: Include accessibility and language access in use case scoring. Test public-facing tools with users before launch. Monitor complaints, failed interactions, correction requests, and access barriers after deployment.
textPreview: Include accessibility and language access in use case scoring. Test public-facing tools with users before launch. Monitor complaints, fai...

text:
Use this slide in the Accessibility and Language Access training module.
Use this slide to turn the concept into practical behaviors, decisions, and safeguards.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 4, 7, 10, 11, 13 and Tools 3, 19, 20, 22, 29, 41, 47.
Invite participants to identify where these practices would need to be documented in their own department.
Start with What Needs Review, then compare it with the other practice areas on the slide.
Use the topic to examine real workflows, not abstract technology.
textPreview: Use the topic to examine real workflows, not abstract technology.

text:
Use this slide in the Accessibility and Language Access training module.
Use this slide to make the topic concrete through public health workflow examples.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 4, 7, 10, 11, 13 and Tools 3, 19, 20, 22, 29, 41, 47.
For each example, ask: What problem is being solved? What data would be used? Who reviews the AI output? What decision remains human? What could go wrong?
Which AI-supported outputs will be public-facing or client-facing? Who reviews translations, accessibility, readability, and cultural context? What non-AI or human pathway remains available?
textPreview: Which AI-supported outputs will be public-facing or client-facing? Who reviews translations, accessibility, readability, and cultural con...

text: Review an AI-generated public health message and identify changes needed for plain language, accessibility, language access, cultural context, and human assistance pathways.

Use this slide in the Accessibility and Language Access training module.
Use this slide for a facilitated activity near the end of the lesson.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 4, 7, 10, 11, 13 and Tools 3, 19, 20, 22, 29, 41, 47.
Give participants time to work individually or in small groups. Ask them to produce a practical artifact that can feed into readiness assessment, governance review, use case prioritization, or implementation planning.
Expected takeaways include: Accessibility and language access review notes, Revised public message, Monitoring indicators for access barriers.
Use this topic with Plays 1, 4, 7, 10, 11, 13 and Tools 3, 19, 20, 22, 29, 41, 47. Turn the discussion into a documented question, decision, gap, or implementation artifact.

Close by connecting the lesson to next actions and resources.
